Okay, step one in interviewing, which is reviewing resumes. All right, so I want to explain to you how to most efficiently do this process so that you get people the fastest as well as you're not wasting your time. All right, so one, when you're doing resumes, you need to look at what you want. Okay, so one, you want to see achievements, right, you want to see that they've done something cool, you want to see that they highlight their achievements, right. Two, their ability to keep a job a job longer term. When I say longer term, in terms of the people that you're looking for, I'm talking maybe one to three years. Okay, and if it is shorter, maybe they just got out of school, they were a student, whatever, right. So there's always exceptions, but I'm saying generally you want someone that's not every two to three months hopping from one job to another, right. Three, an applicant who's self aware enough to know if they've been a bad fit their prior job. So maybe they put notes on there like if they did hop jobs, maybe like wasn't for me, you know, I thought that I would enjoy this didn't right. And lastly is optimism. So maybe they include a cover letter, maybe they're the way they write there's color, they've made a cool resume and it looks nice right. What do you not want? Okay, one, you don't want misspelling resumes, like we have Google, we have spell check, there's really no excuses. One thing I will tell you is that if I find like one mistake, I'm like, okay, I'm going to let slide because everything else looks great. But if I'm constant, like they cannot type or spell, like that's a bit bigger, I've cracked me. To any bad mouthing of prior or current jobs. So speaking to maybe you put questions on your application page that are like, why do you want this job? And they're like, I hate the job I have now. All right, well, they're gonna hate that job too, because they probably hate themselves. So that's not a good person, which then leads to any sort of negativity. You don't want people are negative. If you get negative vibes from a resume, imagine that person in person, right. And then of course, job hopping, you don't want to see people that have had a ton of jobs or excessive jobs in a short period of time. Okay, so that's what you're looking for when you're looking at resumes. Okay. Now here's the sequence of events that you want, which goes from resume screening to interview. Okay, one, you want to highlight and star all the candidates that spark interest to you on whatever medium you have creds list indeed, LinkedIn, whatever it may be. Second is you want to call them immediately for a phone screen. Okay, I'm going to go into that in a second. Third, if they don't answer, you send them an email with a link to schedule. Okay, an interview. If they answer, you want to conduct a phone screen. And if they're a good fit based on the phone screen, then you want to schedule the interview right then. It's like a sale, like you need to lock them in, you need to make the sale. Like the sale is getting them to schedule the interview. Okay. So phone screening 101. What is this? This is just you calling a person to weed out and make sure that there are no like weirdos that have applied or maybe their resume looks great. And then you talk to them and you're like, wow, I really can't picture this working, you know, personality, whatever. So first off is asking the basics. Okay, so basic questions. Why do you apply for this job? What are you looking for full time, part time, etc. Two, you want to gather salary expectations, making sure that what you're offering is what they're looking for because that's a fundamental thing that if it doesn't align, then there's no point in having an interview and you would waste both of your time. Three is gazing their desire for the job. So how badly do they want it? Are they disinterested? Did they even remember the name of your company leading into number four, which is how much do they know about your company? Did they Google you? Did they look up your Facebook? Did they research your name? Right. You want people who are more interested. And then lastly, addressing issues you saw with the resume. So if you saw a lot of job hopping or they changed careers or something of that sort, bring that up as a question and just addressing it early on. Right. So some red flags when your phone's screening, right? One would be a lack of enthusiasm. They sound like they don't care that you called and they don't care if they have an interview, right? And then there's a kind of laissez-faire about it. Two is they ask you no questions. So it, I mean, don't you think it's kind of odd that like this could be there, what they spend the majority of their life doing and yet they have no questions for you? Like, I find that odd. I think you should find it odd too. Third is they sound distracted during the interview, so they are not giving you their full attention, which is disrespectful, right? So, four negative comments about former employees, employers, jobs that they've had, whatever. Something I would mind you of is if somebody says that they know that they were not a good fit for a job they were at and they're self-aware enough to recognize that, that's totally acceptable. A focus on money, so extremely adamant about the fact they're just doing this to make money because that's not going to work well with your culture if you want to have a good purpose driven culture. Lastly is excessive cursing. That obviously is a little bit awkward. So those are the red flags I suggest to look out for on the phone screen.
